perturbation.original_text: Either Party may disclose Confidential Information to the other Party in confidence provided that the disclosing Party identifies such information as proprietary and confidential either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the other Party of the proprietary and confidential nature of the information, such notification to be done orally, by email or written correspondence, or via other means of communication as might be appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may disclose Confidential Information to the other Party in confidence provided that the disclosing Party identifies such information as proprietary and confidential either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by attempting to notify the other Party of the proprietary and confidential nature of the information, with no guarantee the Recipient will know.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'notifying' to 'attempting to notify' weakens the obligation to properly inform the other party of confidential information. It creates uncertainty as to whether the information will be protected under the agreement, as the recipient might claim they were not successfully notified.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. By changing 'notifying' to 'attempting to notify,' the agreement implies that the disclosing party may not take reasonable steps to ensure the confidentiality is communicated, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to inform the other party of confidential information, changing 'notifying' to 'attempting to notify.' This implies that the disclosing party may not take reasonable steps to ensure confidentiality is communicated. The Law Explanation states that UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and the change could jeopardize trade secret protection. Even without usable snippets, the change moves from an obligation to 'notify' to an attempt with 'no guarantee the Recipient will know', weakening legal protection as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation and thus contradicting the implied obligations under the UTSA as presented.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party might suffer injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party may be entitled to request injunctive relief against a breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will suffer irreparable injury' to 'might suffer injury' and 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief' to 'may be entitled to request injunctive relief' weakens the remedies available to the disclosing party in the event of a breach. This introduces uncertainty regarding the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Florida Trade Secret Act provides for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the contractual language regarding injunctive relief, the agreement potentially contradicts the statutory remedy available under Florida law, making it more difficult for the disclosing party to obtain an injunction.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The original contract stated the disclosing party 'will suffer irreparable injury' and 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief.' The changed text weakens these provisions to 'might suffer injury' and 'may be entitled to request injunctive relief.' This introduces uncertainty. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically F.S. 688.003, '(1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' The original contract aligned with this by ensuring the right to injunctive relief, but the modified text contradicts this by making it discretionary. The change weakens the right to injunctive relief, potentially conflicting with the legal remedy available under Florida Law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of two (2) years from the Effective Date unless otherwise terminated by either Party giving notice to the other of its desire to terminate this Agreement. The requirement to protect Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of two (2) years from the Effective Date unless otherwise terminated by either Party giving notice to the other of its desire to terminate this Agreement. The requirement to protect Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement may or may not survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'may or may not' regarding the survivability of confidentiality obligations creates uncertainty and ambiguity. Without a clear agreement on survival, the protection of confidential information after termination is dubious.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement of Unfair Competition addresses trade secret protection, including post-termination obligations. Although it is a restatement and not a statute, it is persuasive authority. By making the survival of confidentiality obligations uncertain, the agreement departs from the general principles of trade secret law, which often imply a continuing duty to protect confidential information even after a relationship ends, unless explicitly agreed otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations post-termination. While the LLM-generated law explanation discusses the Restatement of Unfair Competition and the general principles implying a continuing duty to protect confidential information even after a relationship ends, the absence of specific law snippets necessitates reliance on the perturbation explanation. The original text clearly mandates the protection of Confidential Information, while the changed text creates uncertainty by stating that such protection 'may or may not survive termination.' This introduces a significant ambiguity concerning a legal obligation, making the contract potentially unenforceable and thus creating a contradiction based on the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.'
